entry.title: A calm day in Rio
entry.content:
Bom dia!

We thought we would be one of the first people out by Ipanema beach this morning at 6:30, but there were already so many people jogging, playing beach volleyball and exercising already. It seems like Rio has a very large fitness community.

Ipanema beach at 6:30am

After an early morning swim, we took a walk around the Lagoa Rodrigo de Freitas (a lake in the south zone).

We arrived at Jardim Botanico (The Botanical Gardens) at 11am and lined up to get a ticket.

The gardens had a lot of preserved wildlife as well as a cactus garden.

Botnical garden

After the gardens we bought some quick lunch at the supermarket and had a picnic on Ipanema beach. The beach was full of people, and every 30 seconds there would be a vendor passing by selling all kinds of things from drinks and clothes to food like corn and acai.

Ipanema beach

For dinner, we went to have some Poke bowls right up the street. The owner was extremely friendly and gave us some free ‘deep fried tofu’ - a new dish he had never made.

Poke bowl

We asked him if he had any recommendations for a good caipirinha. He said we should check out the Padeira Ipanema (Ipanema Bakery) where they have a rooftop bar. We were very surprised to find that it was a huge bakery where once you go in, you can walk up to the rooftop (while passing the kitchen) and there is a restaurant/bar. We ordered a cashew + holy grass and seriguela caiprinha. Very interesting flavours. Then we had some ‘Chopp’ and called it a night.

Ipanema Bakery

Chopp

We managed to make it through Rio without getting our phones stolen. Tomorrow we leave for Iguazu Falls.

What to say about Rio after spending three days here? First, the good. Ipanema beach is great. You could spend all day there swimming, eating, drinking, exercising. The number of beach vendors might be a put/off for some, but they were all nice and didn’t intrudeif you just say no. Overall a great time.

The scenery around the city is amazing. Forested mountains all around with steep, rocky sides. And the ocean as well, of course. Going up a mountain to get a view is a must, probably.

Now, the not so good. Rio is loud. Everywhere we went there was construction making noise. And if that wasn’t the case there was the constant sound of the city’s hectic traffic. Going by car felt somewhat unsafe with all the motorcyclists zig-zagging between the cars and honking their horns. Im just glad I didn’t have to drive myself…

The inequality is palpable in Rio. The favelas are nestled in between gated communities. Even in Ipanema, an affluent neighborhood, there were plenty of desperate people living in the street.

Anyway, we had a good time, would come back again!

entry.title: Rio by day
entry.content:
Hello. This message is delivered to you by Kaj.

We were greeted today by another blue sky. The sun has really been smiling since we arrived in Rio yesterday morning.

After some deliberation we decided on two objectives for the day: Sugarloaf Mountain and the Museum of Tomorrow (Museo do Amanha).

We hailed an Uber after breakfast and went headed to the north of Copacabana beach, to the neighborhood of Botafogo. A gondola took us to the first mountain, where we enjoyed som nice views of the city and the surrounding landscape. It’s beautiful!

The gondola!

We boarded a second gondola to get to Sugarloaf Mountain, an imposing rock with steep sides that rises out of the Atlantic. Once up there we were treated to even more amazing views of, among other things, Copacabana Beach. We were also lucky enough to get a glimpse of some wildlife, tiny howler monkeys inhabit the mountain and they were super cute!

The gondola to Sugarloaf Mountain.

Rika made a friend in the little grove at the top of the mountain.

The lizard!

It was approaching noon so we dicided to have som acai. It’s super delicious.

Acai - 10/10 (Rika + Kaj)

For lunch we went to Café Tero in the central city. I had a vegetable curry and Rika went for an aubergine sandwich. It was all very good, albeit a bit salty.

Lunch break!

The curry - 7/10 (Kaj) The sandwich - 8/10 (Rika)

We spent the afternoon in the Museum of Tomorrow. It’s a futuristic building by the water. The exhibition was pretty cool, especially the introductory 360-degree video show. We spent about two hours in the museum, including a visit to the temporary ocean exhibit (which was all in Portuguese). All in all it was a fun experience!

The museum!

The museum!

Before taking the metro back to Ipanema we had som coffee at a cafe.

Dinner was taken at a vegan fast-food place called Acogue Vegano (Vegan Butcher). It was cheap and greasy. Perfect after a long day of sightseeing.

The melt - 6/10 (Rika) The bbq - 7/10 (Kaj)

That’s it! ‘Til next time.

Kaj
entry.summary: Hello. This message is delivered to you by Kaj.

entry.title: Off to Brazil!
entry.content:
We arrived at Arlanda Terminal 2 with plenty of time to spare. Soon after, we received a notification that our flight had been delayed by 30 minutes.

Kaj, before the delay news, happily enjoying the free beer in the lounge:

Thirty minutes turned into forty, then fifty. By the time we finally boarded, it was already 19:15 — more than an hour after our scheduled departure time of 18:10. When 19:40 rolled around and we were still sitting on the runway, we started giving up on the idea of making our connecting flight in Lisbon at 23:25 to Rio. The mood was low, and when Kaj asked the cabin crew if they had any idea about the connection, they said they didn’t know.

Finally, we landed at 23:00 — already past the gate closing time. However, the staff told us to follow the instructions of the people wearing orange vests. As soon as the plane came to a stop (and the seatbelt sign turned off, of course), we grabbed our bags and rushed off the plane.

The airport was nearly empty except for a few staff members in orange vests, who shouted at us to run to border control and then to gate 42A. The border control officer barely looked at our passports before waving us through. Somehow, we made it just in time for the final call.

10 hours across the Atlantic

I’m not sure if it was the unexpected cardio at 23:00 or just pure exhaustion, but both Kaj and I slept through most of the flight. We only woke up about two hours before landing.

View from the window seat:

As for the in-flight games, Kaj beat me at world trivia — I’m convinced it was rigged. After a quick Sudoku session, we finally landed in Rio de Janeiro!

Kaj is a Sudoku master:

Going through border control was surprisingly smooth. We joined the estrangeiros (foreigners) line, and it only took about ten minutes. Once outside, we were immediately bombarded with people asking “Taxi?”, but from prior research we knew the best way to get into the city was by Uber. Surprisingly, there was a designated Uber pickup area, which made things much easier.

Organised Uber pickup spot:

Sweaty but happy at the Uber lot in Rio GIG Airport:

The drive to Ipanema took over an hour. The car ride was… eventful. Let’s just say we felt significantly safer driving in Albania than we did here.

We made it to Ipanema, dropped off our bags, and headed straight for the beach.

Five minutes into the walk, Kaj’s sandal gave up.

The tragedy:

After finding a replacement, we finally made it to the beach.

KAJPIRINHA

Extremely exhausted but happy at Copacabana Beach:

We also found a nice café in Copacabana called Teva Deli. Kaj ordered the cheeseburger with truffle fries, and I got the mushroom Benedict.

Cheeseburger — 6/10 (Kaj)

Truffle fries — 3/10 (Kaj + Rika)

Mushroom Benedict — 8/10 (Rika)

And finally for dinner we had some traditional Brazilian food.

Feijoada: — 7/10 (Rika)

Moqueca — 8/10 (Kaj)

Stay tuned for more Rio Adventures!
entry.summary: Our first stop is in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.

entry.title: 4 more days to go…
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog.

Currently scrambling to get everything we need for our trip!

  • Vaccinations

  • DEET mosquito repellent

  • Shoes for hiking

  • Good humour
